stdout:
=== MAIN RESULT (from Table 1, real data) ===
Male retractions: 8088, total male articles: 12677541
Female retractions: 3534, total female articles: 6809518
RESULT male_RR_per_10000 = 6.38‱  (95% CI: 6.24‱, 6.52‱)
RESULT female_RR_per_10000 = 5.19‱  (95% CI: 5.02‱, 5.36‱)
RESULT MFRR = 1.229  (95% CI: 1.182, 1.279)
PAPER_REPORTED: male RR = 6.38‱, female RR = 5.19‱, MFRR = 1.23 (95% CI: 1.18, 1.28)

!!! The following analysis uses a small SYNTHETIC dataset for illustration only.
    The numbers are NOT the paper's real results; they demonstrate the methodology.
=== Synthetic data overall ===
Male retractions: 49/60000, Female retractions: 20/40000
SYNTHETIC male RR: 8.17‱, female RR: 5.00‱
SYNTHETIC MFRR: 1.633 (95% CI: 0.971, 2.747)

=== MFRR by publication year ===
Year 2008: MFRR=2.716 (95% CI: 0.304, 24.289)
Year 2009: MFRR=1.009 (95% CI: 0.169, 6.032)
Year 2010: MFRR=nan (95% CI: nan, nan)
Year 2011: MFRR=1.328 (95% CI: 0.120, 14.638)
Year 2012: MFRR=0.654 (95% CI: 0.092, 4.640)
Year 2013: MFRR=0.684 (95% CI: 0.171, 2.732)
Year 2014: MFRR=nan (95% CI: nan, nan)
Year 2015: MFRR=1.870 (95% CI: 0.195, 17.968)
Year 2016: MFRR=2.044 (95% CI: 0.213, 19.644)
Year 2017: MFRR=nan (95% CI: nan, nan)
Year 2018: MFRR=1.296 (95% CI: 0.118, 14.287)
Year 2019: MFRR=nan (95% CI: nan, nan)
Year 2020: MFRR=1.993 (95% CI: 0.207, 19.154)
Year 2021: MFRR=1.145 (95% CI: 0.274, 4.787)
Year 2022: MFRR=0.682 (95% CI: 0.096, 4.838)
Year 2023: MFRR=2.001 (95% CI: 0.208, 19.225)

=== MFRR by retraction reason ===
mistakes: MFRR=1.333 (95% CI: 0.538, 3.303), male RR=2.33‱, female RR=1.75‱
fabrication_falsification: MFRR=2.167 (95% CI: 0.707, 6.645), male RR=2.17‱, female RR=1.00‱
duplication: MFRR=9.333 (95% CI: 1.227, 70.976), male RR=2.33‱, female RR=0.25‱
plagiarism: MFRR=0.133 (95% CI: 0.016, 1.141), male RR=0.17‱, female RR=1.25‱
ethical_issues: MFRR=1.333 (95% CI: 0.244, 7.279), male RR=0.67‱, female RR=0.50‱
authorship_issues: MFRR=2.000 (95% CI: 0.208, 19.227), male RR=0.50‱, female RR=0.25‱
single_reason: MFRR=1.633 (95% CI: 0.971, 2.747), male RR=8.17‱, female RR=5.00‱
multiple_reasons: MFRR=nan (95% CI: nan, nan), male RR=0.00‱, female RR=0.00‱

=== MFRR by subject field ===
Biomedical and health sciences: MFRR=2.504 (95% CI: 0.935, 6.705)
Mathematics and computer science: MFRR=1.327 (95% CI: 0.454, 3.881)
Social sciences and humanities: MFRR=1.341 (95% CI: 0.404, 4.452)
Physical sciences and engineering: MFRR=1.219 (95% CI: 0.409, 3.635)
Life and earth sciences: MFRR=2.048 (95% CI: 0.213, 19.676)

=== MFRR by country (top 10 by retracted count) ===
USA: MFRR=1.695 (95% CI: 0.532, 5.402)
China: MFRR=0.778 (95% CI: 0.262, 2.314)
Pakistan: MFRR=6.809 (95% CI: 0.872, 53.163)
Italy: MFRR=3.256 (95% CI: 0.381, 27.859)
Germany: MFRR=2.610 (95% CI: 0.292, 23.344)
Iran: MFRR=0.991 (95% CI: 0.166, 5.928)
UK: MFRR=2.630 (95% CI: 0.294, 23.524)
France: MFRR=1.033 (95% CI: 0.173, 6.177)
Netherlands: MFRR=1.289 (95% CI: 0.117, 14.208)
Egypt: MFRR=0.657 (95% CI: 0.041, 10.503)

=== Cross-analysis: MFRR by field and retraction reason (selected combinations) ===

=== CONCLUSION ===
The analysis (based on the paper's Table 1 real counts) shows that male first authors
have a higher overall retraction rate (6.38‱) compared to female first authors (5.19‱),
with an MFRR of 1.23 (95% CI 1.18–1.28). This is consistent with the paper's finding
that male leading authors retract more articles relative to their publication volume.
The synthetic demonstration reproduces the methodology for all other dimensions.
